description: A patient, in-depth introduction to pointers: what they are, how to declare, assign, dereference, pointer arithmetic, pointers and arrays, pointers to pointers, and const with pointers. Many examples.

sections.title: What is a Pointer?
sections.definition: A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Instead of holding a value directly, it 'points to' where the value lives in memory.
sections.analogy: Think of a house: a normal variable contains the person (value). A pointer contains the address (street number) where that person lives. With the address, you can find the person.
sections.output:
Value of x: 42
Address of x: 0x7ffd5e2b3a1c
Value of ptr (address stored): 0x7ffd5e2b3a1c
Value pointed to by ptr: 42

sections.title: Declaring Pointers
sections.definition: Declare a pointer by adding * after the type. int* p means 'p is a pointer to an integer'. The * binds to the variable name, but spacing doesn't matter.

sections.title: Address-of Operator (&)
sections.definition: The & operator gives the memory address of a variable. Use it to assign an address to a pointer.

sections.title: Dereference Operator (*)
sections.definition: The * operator (when used on a pointer) accesses the value stored at the address the pointer holds. This is called dereferencing.
sections.output:
Before change: value = 42
copy from pointer: 42
After *ptr = 99: value = 99
After (*ptr)++: value = 100

sections.title: Null Pointers
sections.definition: A null pointer points to nothing (address 0). It's good practice to initialize pointers to nullptr (C++11) or NULL to avoid dangling pointers.

sections.title: Pointers and Functions (Pass by Pointer)
sections.definition: Passing a pointer to a function allows the function to modify the original variable (like pass by reference, but explicit).
sections.output:
Before increment: 5
After increment: 6
Before swap: p=10, q=20
After swap: p=20, q=10

sections.title: Pointer Arithmetic
sections.definition: Adding an integer to a pointer moves it by that many elements, not bytes. Incrementing an int* moves by 4 bytes (size of int).
sections.output:
ptr points to: 10
After ptr++: 20
After ptr+=2: 40
After ptr--: 30
Number of elements between: 4
start is before end

sections.title: Pointers and Arrays
sections.definition: Array name is a constant pointer to the first element. arr[i] is equivalent to *(arr + i).

sections.title: Pointers to Pointers (Double Pointers)
sections.definition: A pointer that stores the address of another pointer. Declared with **. Useful for modifying pointer arguments in functions or for 2D dynamic arrays.

sections.title: const and Pointers
sections.definition: const can be applied to the pointer itself or the pointed-to data. Four combinations: pointer to const, const pointer, const pointer to const.

sections.title: Void Pointers (void*)
sections.definition: void* can hold address of any data type, but cannot be dereferenced without casting. Used for generic programming.
